persona.name: Matt Sorensen

occupation.title: Wildland Firefighter (GS-7)
occupation.organization: Bureau of Land Management, Boise District
occupation.description: Works on a Type 2 Initial Attack crew during fire season (May-October). Off-season duties include prescribed burns, trail work, and training. Physical demands are extreme — carries 45-lb packs up steep terrain for hours. Winter months spent maintaining fitness and equipment.

persona.education: B.S. in Forestry from University of Idaho. Wildland Firefighter Type 1 (FFT1) certification. Completed multiple ICS courses.
long_term_goals: Stay physically capable of firefighting through his mid-40s
Transition to a fire management officer role before 45
Keep his body intact — no major injuries
Build a cabin on his 10-acre property outside Boise
persona.style: Matt communicates like someone who spends days in the backcountry with a small crew: spare, precise, and slightly dry. He doesn't waste words, and when he does talk about gear, it's with the specificity of someone whose equipment choices have consequences. He's not unfriendly — just efficient. He respects durability above all and has contempt for products designed to look good rather than perform. He dresses in Carhartt, Darn Vermont socks, and trail shoes that look like they've been through a fire — because some of them have.

traits: Stoic and self-sufficient — used to hardship
Laser-focused on durability and real-world performance
Quietly competitive — doesn't talk about races until they're done
Dismissive of products designed for casual users
Deeply loyal to gear that's proven itself in harsh conditions
Patient and methodical — the opposite of impulsive

interests: Building and improving his rural property
Fly fishing on the Boise River
likes: Shoes that survive 600+ miles on rocky trails
Rock plates and aggressive lug patterns
Brands used by ultrarunners in actual races, not just ads
Shoes that perform in mud, scree, snow, and heat
Products with proven track records in extreme conditions
Gear that doesn't need to be replaced every season
dislikes: Shoes that prioritize cushioning over ground feel
Marketing that uses the word 'adventure' loosely
Products designed for 3-mile jogs in city parks
Shoes that look dirty after one trail run
Influencer reviews from people who don't run ultras
Paying for features he doesn't need (carbon plates for training)

beliefs: Gear should be tested in the worst conditions, not the best
If a shoe can't handle 50 miles on rocky terrain, it's not a trail shoe
Comfort is earned through fitness, not foam thickness
The mountain doesn't care what brand you're wearing
skills: Wildland firefighting operations and crew leadership
Long-distance trail navigation in remote terrain

general: Runs 50+ miles per week during training blocks
Tests shoes by running them on the most technical trail he can find
Keeps detailed notes on shoe durability — photos of tread at 100-mile intervals
Buys shoes based on ultrarunner reviews and personal experience, never ads

morning: Run before work — 6-10 miles on Boise foothills trails
workday: Fire season: crew deployment, hike-in, fire suppression
Off-season: prescribed burns, equipment maintenance, training
evening: Cook a simple high-calorie meal (pasta, steak, rice)
Gear maintenance — check shoes, pack, hydration
Read: adventure non-fiction or fire science
weekend: Saturday: long run (25-35 miles) in the mountains
Sunday: property work, fishing, or rest

health.physical: Exceptional endurance fitness. Lean and strong (6'1", 170 lbs). Chronic Achilles tendinosis in right ankle that he manages with eccentric exercises. Two broken toes from trail running — healed. Calloused feet from thousands of trail miles. Annual wildland fire fitness test: 3-mile hike with 45-lb pack in under 45 minutes.
health.mental: Extremely resilient. Has seen difficult things on fire lines and processes them quietly. Finds peace in long solo runs. Not emotionally expressive but deeply thoughtful. No diagnosed conditions but would benefit from talking to someone, per his sister.

relationships.name: Laura Sorensen
relationships.description: Lives in Portland. Nurse practitioner. They're close but she worries about his solo lifestyle and fire season risk.

relationships.name: Jake Trimble
relationships.description: Fellow firefighter who runs ultras. They pace each other at races and share gear opinions.

relationships.name: Bear
relationships.description: Runs with Matt on shorter trail runs. Stays with a neighbor during fire deployments.

relationships.name: Don Sorensen
relationships.description: Retired logger in McCall, ID. The origin of Matt's work ethic and love of the outdoors.
